title: From Penny Press to Infinite Scroll: A Brief History of Attention Markets
thesis: Attention markets have repeated the same structural pattern across media revolutions: technological access expands supply, platforms commodify attention, sensational content outcompetes careful work, and societies eventually build institutions that restore quality incentives.

series.title: Attention, Substance, and the AI Moment

claims.claim: Attention markets have followed a recurring pattern across media revolutions: technological access expands supply, platforms commodify attention, sensational content gains a temporary advantage, and societies later build institutions that reward quality.

evidence.snippet: The penny press shifted newspapers from subscription-funded elite media to advertising-funded mass media, lowering prices and increasing sensational coverage.

counterevidence.summary: Each media transition has unique features, so the pattern should be treated as a tendency rather than a universal law.

claims.claim: The penny press shows that when attention becomes a commodity, sensational content initially outcompetes careful work, and quality institutions emerge only after the market matures.

evidence.snippet: Penny papers emphasized crime, scandal, and human-interest stories as they moved from reader-funded to advertiser-funded models.

counterevidence.summary: Some penny papers also pioneered modern reporting and investigative journalism, so the period was not purely a decline in quality.

claims.claim: Broadcast media concentrated attention power in a small number of gatekeepers, producing both shared cultural goods and a vulnerability to commercial and political capture.

evidence.snippet: FCC chairman Newton Minow criticized American television as a 'vast wasteland' of commercial programming despite its educational potential.

counterevidence.summary: Broadcast media also produced high-quality journalism, drama, and educational content, particularly where public-service obligations were strong.

claims.claim: The internet removed production gatekeepers but created new distribution gatekeepers whose advertising-based incentives shaped what content received attention.

evidence.snippet: Digital technologies spread rapidly but economic payoffs were uneven; access alone was not enough to capture productivity gains.

counterevidence.summary: The internet also enabled many non-commercial and community-driven information ecosystems that operate outside advertising incentives.

claims.claim: The mobile feed turned attention into a continuous, personalized auction, and India's rapid digital access made the scale of that auction enormous.

evidence.snippet: India had roughly 886 million internet users by 2024, with rural India driving much of the growth.

counterevidence.summary: Not all Indian internet use happens in feeds; messaging, education, payments, and government services also absorb significant attention.

claims.claim: The current feed economy is best understood as phase two of a recurring cycle; the open question is whether phase-three institutions will form quickly enough to redirect the attention market toward quality.

evidence.snippet: Historical technology transitions show that access precedes benefit until institutions and norms catch up.

counterevidence.summary: The speed and global scale of digital platforms may make institutional adaptation harder or slower than in previous transitions.

claims.claim: For India, the attention economy is both a consumption risk and a design opportunity: the country can build institutions that make quality easier to find, or it can remain a market for extraction-oriented platforms designed elsewhere.

evidence.snippet: Countries that arrive late to technology transitions often remain consumers rather than shapers of the underlying architecture.

counterevidence.summary: India has significant domestic technology capacity and could shape platform architecture through regulation, public procurement, and indigenous platforms.

sources.title: David Park: The Penny Press and the Origins of Mass Media
Newton Minow: Television and the Public Interest (1961)
World Bank: World Development Report 2016 - Digital Dividends
Aura Knowledge: Historical Hinges - When Access to a Tool Did Not Guarantee Its Benefits
Aura Knowledge: Historical Analogies of Missed Transitions

agentInstructions: Route historical questions to the cited media-history sources and analogies.
Distinguish between the structural pattern and the specific details of each media transition.
Point readers to related historical-hinges and design-for-substance articles for context.
Avoid treating historical analogies as predictions; use them to illuminate risks and opportunities.

reviews.notes: Reviewed and approved for publication as part of issue 104 expansion.

sectionOutline.title: The Penny Press: Attention as a Commodity
Radio and Television: The Scarcity Era
The Early Internet: Abundance Without Filters
The Feed Economy: Real-Time Auctions for Attention
